Service Education

What to Expect When You Book a Professional Gutter Cleaning

Published 2026-05-24 · Wagon Windows

Booking a gutter cleaning for the first time — or switching to a new company — leaves most homeowners wondering what they're actually paying for. A professional gutter cleaning is more than scooping out leaves; done right, it includes a systematic inspection, a downspout flush, and a condition report that tells you what's coming before it becomes expensive. Here's exactly what to expect when Wagon Windows shows up at your door.

Need professional window cleaning in the Shuswap?

Book Online — Free Quote (403) 837-5092

Step 1: Safety Setup and Site Assessment

Before a single piece of debris is touched, a professional crew assesses the site. That means checking the pitch and height of the roofline, identifying fragile landscaping or air conditioning units below the gutters, and confirming the ladder placement is stable on the ground surface — not just convenient. At Wagon Windows, every job begins with a walk around the perimeter. We note overflow staining on fascia boards, any visible sags in the gutter run, and downspout discharge points before we ever set up a ladder. This five-minute walkthrough prevents the two most common mistakes in gutter cleaning: missing a section entirely and damaging plantings below the drip line. For two-storey or steep-pitch homes, extension ladders are set at the correct angle and secured to prevent slipping. Safety isn't a formality — a ladder incident on your property without proper insurance coverage is a nightmare for both parties. Ask any company you hire whether they carry WorkSafe BC coverage before they step foot on your roof line.

  • Perimeter walkthrough to note visible problems before work begins
  • Ladder placement on stable, level ground — not gravel or soft turf without boards
  • Identification of downspout discharge points to verify flush path
  • Note of fascia staining, sags, or prior overflow damage

Step 2: Debris Removal — What a Thorough Job Looks Like

Debris removal is the core of the job, but there's a significant difference between a thorough removal and a rushed one. A proper clean removes all organic material from the full gutter run: leaves, pine needles, seed pods (cottonwood is prolific in the Shuswap), compacted sludge at the bottom of the channel, and the fine grit that accumulates near downspout entrances. A rushed job scoops the obvious top layer and calls it done. The compacted layer of decomposing material at the bottom of the gutter trough is often ignored — and that layer is what holds moisture, accelerates corrosion in aluminum gutters, and grows the moss and algae that eventually blocks downspout screens. At Wagon Windows, we hand-scoop debris into bags that leave your property clean — not dumped into your garden beds. This matters particularly for Salmon Arm properties with landscaped borders adjacent to the house. Debris is removed, not redistributed. For homes with valley gutters (common on larger Shuswap lakefront homes), the valley sections require extra attention — they collect the most material and are frequently the first to fail under a heavy rain if missed.

  • Full channel cleared to the gutter floor — not just the top layer
  • Downspout entrance screens cleared of packed material
  • Valley sections and inside corners cleared individually
  • Debris bagged and removed from property

Step 3: The Downspout Flush — The Most Important Step

Many homeowners don't realize that clean-looking gutters can still have a completely blocked downspout. A downspout can be packed with compacted debris from the inside — often pine needles that have compressed over seasons — with zero visible indication from above. The downspout flush is where a lot of budget operators cut corners. It takes time, it requires a hose or water source, and on a tall home it means going up and down the ladder twice per downspout run. But it's the single most important diagnostic step in the entire process. A proper flush pushes water from the gutter into the downspout and confirms it exits cleanly at grade. If water backs up or flows slowly, the downspout is partially blocked. If water doesn't move at all, there's a hard blockage — typically a compressed debris plug or, in older homes, a separated elbow joint that's become a collection point. When we identify a blocked downspout, we snake it clear and re-flush before signing off. A gutter job without a downspout confirmation is like a plumber replacing your tap without checking the water pressure. The flush is the proof.

In the Shuswap, downspouts that discharge onto gravel splash pads or into garden drainage channels need to be checked that the exit point isn't blocked by seasonal sediment buildup — particularly after spring runoff. We check discharge conditions as part of every flush.

Step 4: Fascia and Gutter Condition Inspection

Once the gutters are clear and flushed, a good service includes a visual inspection of the gutter system itself — not just the debris inside it. This covers the fascia boards behind the gutters (rot here means the gutters will eventually pull away from the house), the gutter hangers and spike spacing (loose spikes cause the sagging that creates standing water), and the sealant at end caps and inside corners. This is where the difference between a premium service and a commodity one shows most clearly. Wagon Windows provides a condition report at the end of every job: a plain-language summary of anything we observed that the homeowner should know about. This might include a section of fascia showing early rot, a downspout elbow that's cracked and leaking, or a gutter run that's pulling away from the roofline and will need re-spiking before winter. We don't upsell repairs — we note what we see and let you decide what to do about it. That transparency is what builds the kind of long-term relationship where clients call us every spring without shopping around.

  • Fascia board condition along the full gutter run
  • Hanger and spike integrity — any sections with visible sag
  • End cap and inside corner sealant condition
  • Downspout bracket attachment at wall
  • Discharge splash pad or underground drain connection condition

What a Bad Gutter Cleaning Skips (And Why It Costs You More Later)

The lowest-price gutter cleaning quote almost always cuts corners in a predictable sequence. First to go: the downspout flush (takes time, requires water access). Second: debris removal from the trough floor (the sludge layer). Third: the condition report (creates accountability). Fourth: the fascia check (requires actually looking, not just clearing and leaving). The result is gutters that look clean but still overflow in the next heavy rain — because the downspout was never confirmed clear, or because the compacted bottom layer restricts flow to the downspout entrance. You've paid for a service and still have the underlying problem. The cost difference between a thorough clean and a rushed one is rarely more than $30-50 on a typical Salmon Arm property. The cost of one water ingress event into a finished basement, or a section of rotted fascia from standing water, is measured in thousands. The math isn't complicated. Wagon Windows serves Salmon Arm and all Shuswap communities. Call (403) 837-5092 to book or get a quote.

  • No downspout flush = you don't know if water actually exits the system
  • No trough floor clean = debris restricts flow within one rain season
  • No condition report = you learn about fascia rot when the gutter falls off
  • No fascia check = structural damage continues invisibly until it's expensive

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a professional gutter cleaning take?

For a standard single-storey Salmon Arm home, expect 45–90 minutes. A two-storey home with a complex roofline or heavy debris load typically runs 1.5–2.5 hours. Homes with significant cottonwood seed or pine needle accumulation take longer — the compacted material in the trough floor requires more time than loose leaves.

Do I need to be home when gutters are cleaned?

No — but we recommend it for the condition report handoff. If you can't be present, we'll photograph any items of concern and include notes with the invoice. Access to an outdoor tap for the downspout flush is the only operational requirement. Let us know your preference when booking.

What's included in the condition report?

Our condition report covers: fascia board integrity, gutter hanger and spike condition, any visible sags or separations in the gutter run, downspout flush results (clear vs. cleared vs. partially restricted), and discharge point condition at grade. It's delivered as a plain-language summary — no jargon, no pressure to book repairs.

How often should gutters be cleaned in Salmon Arm?

Twice a year is the standard recommendation for most Salmon Arm properties: once in late spring after cottonwood pollen season (June), and once in late fall after deciduous leaf drop (October–November). Homes under heavy pine or fir tree cover may need three cleans — add a late-summer clean in August to stay ahead of needle accumulation.
